content:
Captivity doesn’t just annoy the animal; it changes the meat. (created by author)
The old man chewed once, twice, stopped, and put his fork down.
This was at an asado last month, early winter night here in Patagonia, coals ticking under a sky the color of wet slate. The cut was expensive, a wide bife de chorizo my friend Tomas, the asador, had bragged about all afternoon, blistered black outside, bleeding rose inside, salt crust cracking like thin ice. The old man, a neighbor who worked cattle in the south his whole life and who usually joins these gatherings, held one bite in his mouth the way a sommelier holds a bad Malbec.
Then he delivered the verdict: one word, with the finality that starts and ends a conversation.
“Feedlot.”
He didn’t mean it was rotten or burnt or undercooked. He meant it tasted generic, like the animal had never really lived. A pasture steer covers kilometers a day to feed itself, and all that seeking gets into the muscle, grassy, a little wild, different from one animal to the next. A feedlot steer stands in a pen and the ration comes to it. It gains weight efficiently. It’s cheaper, faster and it tastes like the pen: a photocopy of soft, fatty beef. We Argentines fight about…
